title: Image Fundamentals — Pixels, Channels, Color Spaces

questions.question: A file on disk is decoded into a NumPy array with shape (224, 224, 3) and dtype uint8. What does each number represent?
options: The probability that a pixel is that colour, between 0 and 1
One photon count per detector, normalized to the full dynamic range of the sensor
A sample of light intensity at one grid position, quantized to one of 256 levels per channel
A floating-point brightness value ready to feed to a convolutional network
questions.explanation: A uint8 image stores 8-bit integers in [0, 255]. Each integer is one quantized sample of light intensity at one (row, column, channel) position. It is neither a probability nor a float; you must divide by 255 and standardize before feeding it to a pretrained model.

questions.question: The lesson's `synthetic_rgb` function returns an array `arr` of shape (H, W, 3). After calling `arr_chw = arr.transpose(2, 0, 1)`, what is the new shape and why?
options: (3, H, W) — axis 2 moves to position 0, giving the CHW layout that PyTorch Conv2d expects
(H, 3, W) — channels are moved to the middle axis
(H, W, 3) — transpose(2, 0, 1) on a rank-3 array with last dim 3 is a no-op
(W, H, 3) — the spatial dimensions are swapped
questions.explanation: np.transpose(arr, (2, 0, 1)) maps axis 0→1, 1→2, 2→0, so (H, W, C) becomes (C, H, W). This is the CHW layout cuDNN and PyTorch Conv2d expect. The HWC→CHW axis reorder is the single most common shape transformation in any vision pipeline.

questions.question: In `rgb_to_grayscale`, the function computes `(rgb.astype(float32) @ weights)` with `weights = [0.299, 0.587, 0.114]`. Why is the green weight (0.587) roughly five times larger than the blue weight (0.114)?
options: To compensate for JPEG compression losses that corrupt the blue channel more than green
Because green photons carry more energy than blue photons at the quantum level
Because the Bayer mosaic physically has twice as many green pixels as blue pixels
Because human vision has much higher luminous efficiency for green than for blue — the ITU-R BT.601 weights match perceived brightness, not sensor response
questions.explanation: The ITU-R BT.601 coefficients 0.299/0.587/0.114 reflect the spectral sensitivity of human cone cells. Green occupies the peak of the eye's luminance response curve; blue sits at the far tail. An equal-weight average produces images that look too dim for green-tinted scenes and too bright for blue ones when viewed by a human.

questions.question: In `preprocess_imagenet`, the three steps are (1) divide by 255, (2) subtract ImageNet mean and divide by std, (3) transpose HWC→CHW. Which ordering mistake would silently produce numerically wrong values sent to the model?
options: Transposing first — the reshape would change which channel each mean value is subtracted from

Subtracting the mean before dividing by 255 — you would subtract fractional values near 0.5 from integers in [0, 255], placing the result in the range [-0.5, 254.5] rather than the standardized [-2, +2] the model expects
Any ordering produces identical results because the operations are all affine
Skipping the transpose — PyTorch Conv2d auto-detects axis order at runtime
questions.explanation: The mean values (e.g. 0.485) are defined in [0, 1] space, so the division by 255 must happen first. If you subtract first, you subtract 0.485 from pixel values like 210 and get ~209.5 instead of ~0.44, so the final standardized tensor is about 255× too large. The model receives noise instead of a properly scaled input.

questions.question: Why do ImageNet pretrained models expect inputs standardized with mean=[0.485, 0.456, 0.406] and std=[0.229, 0.224, 0.225]?
options: Those are the RGB values of an average natural scene
Those are the per-channel mean and standard deviation of the ImageNet training set in [0, 1] space, so subtracting them centers the distribution the model was trained on
They make the input strictly zero-mean unit-variance for any image
They are required by the ReLU activation function
questions.explanation: The numbers are statistics of the ImageNet training corpus computed after dividing pixels by 255. Using them aligns your input distribution with the one the network saw during training. For a model trained on a different dataset you would recompute the stats on that dataset.

questions.question: You resize a segmentation mask (integer class IDs 0..20) from 500×500 to 224×224. Which interpolation method is correct?
options: Bilinear — it smooths the class boundaries
Bicubic — it preserves sharpness
Lanczos — highest quality
Nearest neighbour — it preserves valid integer class IDs instead of inventing fractional ones
questions.explanation: Bilinear, bicubic, and lanczos average neighbouring values. On a class-ID mask that produces non-integer values like 4.7 between class 4 and class 5, which are not real classes. Nearest neighbour picks the closest original pixel and keeps the label space intact. This rule also applies to any channel that encodes IDs or indices.
